items.title: OpenAI shipped a cyber defense stack
items.oneliner: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5-Cyber and says Daybreak scanned 30M commits, pushing AI from bug finding into remediation.

items.title: GLM-5.2 made open models harder to dismiss
items.oneliner: Z.ai's 744B GLM-5.2 is being benchmarked against Opus-class models, making local-first frontier AI newly plausible.

items.title: A 3B reasoning model punched up
items.oneliner: VibeThinker-3B reports 94.3 on AIME26, another sign small reasoning models are compressing expensive capability fast.

items.title: Sakana turned orchestration into the product
items.oneliner: Fugu routes requests across model pools through one API, treating model selection itself as a learned frontier capability.

items.title: Tiny inpainting models got serious
items.oneliner: Moebius claims 0.22B parameters with 10B-class inpainting quality under 30ms, which is edge deployment catnip.

items.title: Baidu pushed OCR past page limits
items.oneliner: Unlimited OCR targets one-shot long-horizon parsing, a practical upgrade for document AI teams drowning in messy layouts.

items.title: AI bug hunting is becoming benchmarkable
items.oneliner: Mythos-inspired benchmarking suggests automated vulnerability discovery is maturing from demos into something teams can actually compare.

items.title: Google changed Gemini's default API
items.oneliner: Google made the Interactions API the Gemini default, adding async execution and a remote Linux sandbox for agents.

items.title: SpaceX leased out a giant GPU block
items.oneliner: Reflection AI rented $6.3B of Nvidia compute from SpaceX, showing frontier training is now a supply-chain negotiation.

items.title: Valve's pricing exposed AI hardware pressure
items.oneliner: Valve priced Steam Machines at $1,049 to $1,349 and blamed component volatility, another downstream cost of AI demand.
